title: Alcoholic Hepatitis
oneLiner: Acute hepatocyte injury in heavy drinkers causing jaundice, fever, and acute-on-chronic liver failure — high mortality if missed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old male known for heavy alcohol use (e.g., two bottles of wine daily for 10 years) presents with progressive jaundice over 2–3 weeks, low-grade fever, tender hepatomegaly, and anorexia. He looks unwell, with stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema). His body temperature is 38.0°C, heart rate 95 bpm, and he has mild confusion. Labs show AST > ALT (ratio ~2:1), bilirubin > 5 mg/dL, leukocytosis (neutrophils), and prolonged INR.

**Dissect the Vignette – Pattern Clues:**

- **Heavy alcohol use** → underlying chronic alcohol-related liver disease; acute-on-chronic injury.
- **Progressive jaundice over weeks** → not obstructive (which would be more rapid or painless) and not viral (which may have prodrome).
- **Fever & leukocytosis** → mimics infection, but here due to sterile neutrophilic infiltration of the liver; use discriminant function (DF) to gauge severity.
- **AST > ALT, ratio ~2:1** → hallmark of alcoholic hepatitis; ALT < 300 IU/L typically (deficient pyridoxal phosphate).
- **Tender hepatomegaly** → inflammation stretches capsule; distinguishes from decompensated cirrhosis (which may have shrunken liver).
- **Mild confusion** → early hepatic encephalopathy, often precipitated by infection or active hepatitis.
- **Elevated INR** → synthetic dysfunction, reflecting severity; not solely due to vitamin K deficiency (vitamin K trial fails).
- **Bilirubin > 10 mg/dL often** → poor prognosis if rising despite abstinence.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Painless jaundice with no organomegaly** – mistaken for extrahepatic obstruction. Check ultrasound: no dilated ducts, but may still have gallstones. The clue: heavy alcohol use + AST>ALT ratio.
- **Acute decompensation in a known cirrhotic** – no fever, minimal jaundice; but alcoholic hepatitis often underdiagnosed in decompensated cirrhosis. Look for rapid rise in bilirubin over 1–2 weeks.
- **Normal AST/ALT** – rare but possible in end-stage liver disease with very low hepatic function. Still consider if history is strong; check GGT (elevated) and bilirubin.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Alcoholic hepatitis is an acute inflammatory injury of the liver caused by heavy alcohol consumption. It sits on a spectrum between simple steatosis and cirrhosis – but unlike fatty liver, it carries a risk of rapid liver failure and death.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Up to 35% of heavy drinkers will develop alcoholic hepatitis at some point; many present to hospital with jaundice.
- **Stakes:** In-hospital mortality ~20–30% for severe disease (Maddrey DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20). If missed, patient can die from hepatorenal syndrome, variceal bleeding, or sepsis.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosed as cholangitis, viral hepatitis, or drug-induced liver injury; empiric antibiotics often started unnecessarily, delaying steroids. Also, failure to calculate severity scores leads to under-treatment.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of Disease Development:**  
Chronic alcohol consumption >40–60 g/day (≈2–3 drinks) for >5 years leads to hepatocyte steatosis. In a subset of patients, a “second hit” (e.g., oxidative stress, acetaldehyde adducts, endotoxemia from gut permeability) triggers a neutrophilic inflammatory response. This is alcoholic hepatitis.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

1. **Acetaldehyde toxicity + CYP2E1 induction** → generation of reactive oxygen species → hepatocyte necrosis → **↑ AST (mitochondrial) and ALT (cytoplasmic)**. Due to pyridoxal-P deficiency from alcohol, ALT is suppressed, giving AST>ALT ratio.

2. **Neutrophil infiltration** (chemotaxis by IL-8, complement activation) → **fever and leukocytosis**. This is sterile – no infection necessary. The neutrophilia correlates with severity.

3. **Hepatocyte ballooning & Mallory-Denk bodies** → **loss of synthetic function** → ↓ albumin, ↑ INR (factor VII first). This explains **jaundice** (bilirubin overload from defective conjugation/excretion) and **coagulopathy**.

4. **Portal hypertension** worsens acutely (sinusoidal inflammation + hepatocyte swelling) → **ascites, splenomegaly**. The swollen liver stretches Glisson’s capsule → **tender hepatomegaly**.

5. **Systemic inflammation** (TNF-α, IL-6) → **anorexia, malaise, low-grade fever**. This also triggers **acute kidney injury** (functional prerenal → hepatorenal syndrome) if severe.

6. **Hepatic encephalopathy** – ammonia bypasses damaged liver; also inflammatory cytokines increase brain sensitivity.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**  
The vignette of fever + jaundice + tender liver in a drinker is the direct result of neutrophilic hepatitis. The AST>ALT ratio is specific to this condition (due to pyridoxal-P deficiency). Leukocytosis without obvious infection should immediately raise suspicion for severe alcoholic hepatitis, not start antibiotics.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Exogenous** | **Alcohol** (most common) – chronic heavy intake >40–60 g/day for ≥5 years, often recent binge | “He admits to two bottles of wine daily.” |
| **Genetic / Host** | Female sex, obesity, underlying cirrhosis, genetic polymorphisms (PNPLA3, MBOAT7) | “She is obese and drinks only 3–4 units/day but has fatty liver.” |
| **Co-factors** | Smoking, metabolic syndrome, hepatitis C co-infection | “He has been HCV-positive and drinks heavily.” |
| **Infectious Precipitant** | Gut-derived endotoxin (LPS) – triggers TLR4 signalling | “Recent urinary tract infection .” |
| **Idiopathic** | Some without obvious heavy intake – possibly due to genetic vulnerability | “Denies heavy drinking but has AST>ALT ratio 2:1.” (Rare; be cautious) |

**Most Common Cause:** Chronic heavy alcohol consumption – but severity can be triggered by a recent binge or infection. No other common cause produces the same pattern.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria & Scoring Systems:**

- **Maddrey’s Discriminant Function (DF):** 4.6 × (PT – control PT in seconds) + serum bilirubin (mg/dL).  
  - DF <32: mild/moderate (mortality <5%).  
  - DF ≥32: severe (mortality ~30% at 28 days); consider steroids.
- **MELD score:** More accurate for prognosis (bilirubin, INR, creatinine).  
  - MELD ≥20 indicates high risk.
- **Lille Score** (if on steroids): day 7 – combine age, bilirubin, albumin, creatinine, DF.  
  - Lille <0.45 = responder; >0.45 = non-responder → stop steroids.

**Each Test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| **LFT (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT)** | Hepatocellular vs cholestatic; AST/ALT ratio | Confirm pattern; exclude obstruction | AST <300, AST/ALT >1.5 (usually >2) = alcoholic hepatitis. ALP may be mildly elevated. GGT elevated (but not specific). |
| **Bilirubin** | Jaundice severity; DF & MELD component | Prognostication | Rising >10 mg/dL in <2 weeks = severe. |
| **PT/INR** | Synthetic function | DF calculation; bleeding risk | INR >1.5 without other cause (Vit K deficiency usually less). |
| **Creatinine** | Renal function | MELD; screen for hepatorenal syndrome | Rising Cr = worse prognosis – may need vasopressors. |
| **CBC (WBC)** | Inflammation | Assess for infection vs sterile neutrophilia | Neutrophilia >10,000 very common; if >15,000 with bands, consider sepsis. But also check CRP. |
| **CRP** | Inflammatory marker | Differentiate from sepsis? (limited) | Usually high (>50 mg/L) in alcoholic hepatitis; but if very high (>150), consider infection. |
| **Ultrasound abdomen** | Liver size, fatty infiltration, exclude obstruction | Rule out biliary obstruction, ascites | No dilated ducts; may show fatty liver and ascites. |
| **Transjugular liver biopsy** | Histology: ballooning, Mallory-Denk, neutrophils | Gold standard but invasive; used when diagnosis uncertain | Not routine – reserved for unclear cases. |

**Single confirmatory finding?** No single test confirms; the triad of heavy alcohol history, AST/ALT >2:1 with AST <300, and neutrophilic leukocytosis ± fever makes the diagnosis highly likely. **Rule out** if patient has no alcohol history or if ALT >500 (think viral hepatitis or DILI).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour):**

- **Stop alcohol –** discuss with patient; offer withdrawal prophylaxis (CIWA protocol – chlordiazepoxide 10–50 mg PO q4h prn). Avoid benzodiazepines if severe hepatic encephalopathy.
- **Calculate DF and MELD –** to stratify severity.
- **Check for infection –** chest X-ray, urine culture, blood cultures. Fever + leukocytosis doesn’t automatically mean sepsis – but if high suspicion (e.g., bands, high procalcitonin), start antibiotics after cultures.
- **Give vitamin K 10 mg IV stat** – to correct any INR due to malnutrition (though rarely enough).

**Ward (24–72h):**

- **Nutrition –** enteral feeding strongly preferred; high-calorie, high-protein (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day). Avoid prolonged NPO.
- **Steroids for severe disease:**
  - Indication: DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20 (excluding active infection, GI bleeding, renal failure).
  - Regimen: Prednisolone 40 mg PO daily for 28 days (or methylprednisolone IV if cannot take oral). Do not use if active infection – must be treated first.
  - Monitor Lille score at day 7: if >0.45, stop steroids (no benefit, worse infection risk).
- **Pentoxifylline:** Historically used, but now not recommended (trials showed no survival benefit).
- **Antibiotics:** Only if documented infection. Prophylactic antibiotics not indicated (risk of fungal overgrowth, *C. diff*).
- **Low threshold for screening for varices** (if no known cirrhosis). Prophylactic beta-blockers not started acutely.
- **Ascites:** Paracentesis if tense; check SAAG >1.1, low protein, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) – give albumin if SBP or diuretic-resistant.

**Long-Term:**

- **Alcohol abstinence –** most important modifiable factor. Offer referral to addiction services, naltrexone (50–100 mg PO daily, avoid in decompensated liver? caution) or acamprosate. Campral is safe in mild-mod liver disease.
- **Vaccination:** Hepatitis A & B, pneumococcus, influenza.
- **Surveillance for HCC:** Ultrasound every 6 months if cirrhosis present.
- **Avoid hepatotoxins:** Acetaminophen ≤2 g/day, NSAIDs (renal risk), statins carefully.
- **Consider liver transplant evaluation** if non-responsive despite abstinence for 6 months (select centers offer early transplant for severe alcoholic hepatitis).

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Don’t start antibiotics just for fever + leukocytosis – reassess after cultures. Sterile neutrophilia is common.
- Don’t wait for biopsy to start steroids if DF ≥32 – higher mortality if delayed.
- Don’t give steroids if GI bleeding is ongoing (risk of perforation).
- Don’t use Vitamin K to “fix” INR in severe liver disease – it won’t work because factor synthesis is impaired.
- Don’t discharge without discussing alcohol cessation – relapse rate >50% without support.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Alcoholic Hepatitis | Acute Viral Hepatitis (Hep B) | Acute Cholangitis | Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Mild RUQ, tender hepatomegaly | Often absent or mild RUQ | Severe RUQ pain, colicky, radiating to back | Variable, often none |
| **Exam** | Jaundice, fever, stigmata of CLD, ascites | Jaundice, no ascites (acute), may have hepatomegaly | Jaundice, fever, right upper quadrant tenderness, positive Murphy’s, signs of cholangitis (Charcot’s triad) | Jaundice, may have rash or eosinophilia (if allergic) |
| **ECG** | Nonspecific (sinus tachycardia) | Normal | May show tachycardia | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | AST>ALT (ratio >2), AST <300, ↑GGT, ↑Neutrophils | ALT>AST (ratio <1), AST may be very high (>500), serology: HBsAg+, IgM anti-HBc | ↑ALP, ↑GGT, ↑Bilirubin (direct), ↑WBC with left shift, LFTs cholestatic | Variable; mixed pattern, may have eosinophilia, INR may be elevated |
| **Key Diff** | Heavy alcohol history + AST/ALT ratio >2 + Neutrophilia | Serology positive; ALT >500; no alcohol history | Dilated bile ducts on US; Charcot’s triad (pain, fever, jaundice) | Temporal link to drug; often hepatocellular (ALT > AST) or cholestatic; acute onset |

**Mimic 4: Decompensated cirrhosis without acute hepatitis** – no fever, minimal AST/ALT change, more chronic stigmata, bilirubin often lower but rising gradually.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Ascites / SBP** | Increasing abdominal girth, abdominal pain, fever | Shifting dullness, paracentesis: SAAG >1.1, neutrophils >250/μL | Paracentesis; if SBP: third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime 2g IV q8h) + albumin 1.5 g/kg IV day 1, then 1 g/kg day 3 |
| **Variceal Bleeding** | Haematemesis, melena, hypotension | Stigmata of portal hypertension; emergency OGD | Resuscitate, octreotide 50 μg bolus + 50 μg/h infusion, antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV), band ligation |
| **Hepatic Encephalopathy** | Confusion, asterixis, sleep-wake reversal, coma | Flapping tremor, ammonia elevated (not diagnostic of severity), precipitant: infection, GI bleed, electrolyte | Lactulose 20 g PO/NG q6h (orally/pr), adjust to 2–3 soft stools; rifaximin if non-responsive; treat precipitant |
| **Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS)** | Oliguria, rising creatinine/urea, hyponatraemia | No proteinuria, urine sodium <10, no improvement with volume expansion | Stop diuretics; give albumin 20–40 g/day; if HRS type I: terlipressin (or norepinephrine) + albumin; consider TIPS or transplant |
| **Infections (bacterial/fungal)** | Any change in vitals, worsening encephalopathy | High CRP, procalcitonin, positive cultures | Empiric antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) if systemic signs; fungal prophylaxis with fluconazole if steroids used and high risk |
| **Pancreatitis** | Epigastric pain, vomiting, lipase >3x normal | Co-existing acute pancreatitis (common in alcoholics) | Supportive: NPO, IVF, avoid steroids if pancreatitis is acute (relative contraindication) |

**Late-presentation patterns (stems testing “what if untreated”):**  
- Jaundice worsens, but patient develops ascites and confusion → hepatic encephalopathy + HRS → oliguria and anuria → death from multi-organ failure.  
- Or first bleed from varices → massive haematemesis → shock, aspiration → death.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **AH – Alcoholic Hepatitis Assessment:**
   - **A** – Alcohol history (chronic heavy)
   - **S** – Symptom onset (weeks)
   - **T** – Transaminases AST>ALT
   - **E** – Encephalopathy? / Elevated INR
   - **R** – Ratio (AST/ALT >2)
   
2. **SEVERE** for risk factors:  
   - **S** – Female sex  
   - **E** – Elevated bilirubin (>10)  
   - **V** – Variceal bleeding (or portal HT)  
   - **E** – Elevated INR (>1.5)  
   - **R** – Renal failure  
   - **E** – Encephalopathy

3. **Maddrey DF calculation: 4.6 × (PT – control) + bilirubin (mg/dL)** – remember 4.6 is like “four six-pack” of beer.

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

- **AST > ALT with AST <300** is *almost* pathognomonic for alcoholic hepatitis. Viral hepatitis usually >500.
- **Fever + leukocytosis + jaundice** in a drinker: do not start antibiotics – first check DF. Sterile neutrophilia responds to steroids, not antibiotics.
- **If INR does not correct after vitamin K** and bilirubin is high, think alcoholic hepatitis, not simple cholestasis.
- **Spider naevi + palmar erythema** = chronic liver disease; acute alcoholic hepatitis adds acute deterioration.
- **A rising bilirubin despite improvement in other parameters** may indicate early sepsis (SBP) rather than worsening hepatitis.
- **Steroids only if no active infection** – always screen with CXR, urine, blood cultures, ascitic tap before starting.
- **Transjugular biopsy is reserved** when diagnosis is unclear (e.g., no obvious alcohol history, atypical labs). Most cases diagnosed clinically.

**Common Exam/Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Using ALT >500 to rule out alcoholic hepatitis – viral hepatitis must be excluded first.
- Giving steroids to a patient with GI bleeding – increased risk of perforation.
- Relying on pentoxifylline – now shown to be ineffective.
- Not calculating Lille score at day 7 – many unnecessary prolonged steroid courses.
- Forgetting alcohol withdrawal prophylaxis: seizures can be fatal in a patient with coagulopathy.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥7** – high risk of deterioration; escalate to critical care review.
- **GCS ≤12** – indicates severe encephalopathy; risk of airway compromise → ICU for intubation.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests severe tissue hypoperfusion (consider sepsis or bleeding).
- **Creatinine rising >0.3 mg/dL per day** – early HRS; needs senior review (nephrology/hepatology).
- **DF ≥32 and not responding to steroids** (Lille >0.45, bilirubin not dropping) – consider second-line therapies (e.g., G-CSF? early transplant discussion).
- **Active variceal bleeding** – call endoscopy immediately (out-of-hours: on-call GI team). If hemodynamic instability, involve ICU.
- **INR >2.5 with active bleeding** – reversal needed: FFP or prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) – but avoid over-correction.
- **SBP confirmed** – if patient becomes hypotensive, escalate for vasopressors.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy grade III/IV** – ICU admission for airway protection.
- **Any new-onset atrial fibrillation** (common in alcoholic hepatitis from electrolyte disturbance or alcohol withdrawal) – cardiology consult.

**Specific Thresholds to Remember:**
- MELD ≥20: consider steroids + ICU monitoring.
- DF ≥32: severe – start steroids, consider ICU if other organ failures.
- Lille score after 7 days >0.45: stop steroids – no benefit and increased infection risk.
- Bilirubin plateau >20 mg/dL for >2 weeks: likely non-response; arrange transplant evaluation.

**Always have a low threshold to involve a hepatologist or transplant centre early – severe alcoholic hepatitis is a high-mortality emergency, not a “just stop drinking” condition.**

rawMarkdown:
## Alcoholic Hepatitis

**One-liner:** Acute hepatocyte injury in heavy drinkers causing jaundice, fever, and acute-on-chronic liver failure — high mortality if missed.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 45-year-old male known for heavy alcohol use (e.g., two bottles of wine daily for 10 years) presents with progressive jaundice over 2–3 weeks, low-grade fever, tender hepatomegaly, and anorexia. He looks unwell, with stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema). His body temperature is 38.0°C, heart rate 95 bpm, and he has mild confusion. Labs show AST > ALT (ratio ~2:1), bilirubin > 5 mg/dL, leukocytosis (neutrophils), and prolonged INR.

**Dissect the Vignette – Pattern Clues:**

- **Heavy alcohol use** → underlying chronic alcohol-related liver disease; acute-on-chronic injury.
- **Progressive jaundice over weeks** → not obstructive (which would be more rapid or painless) and not viral (which may have prodrome).
- **Fever & leukocytosis** → mimics infection, but here due to sterile neutrophilic infiltration of the liver; use discriminant function (DF) to gauge severity.
- **AST > ALT, ratio ~2:1** → hallmark of alcoholic hepatitis; ALT < 300 IU/L typically (deficient pyridoxal phosphate).
- **Tender hepatomegaly** → inflammation stretches capsule; distinguishes from decompensated cirrhosis (which may have shrunken liver).
- **Mild confusion** → early hepatic encephalopathy, often precipitated by infection or active hepatitis.
- **Elevated INR** → synthetic dysfunction, reflecting severity; not solely due to vitamin K deficiency (vitamin K trial fails).
- **Bilirubin > 10 mg/dL often** → poor prognosis if rising despite abstinence.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- **Painless jaundice with no organomegaly** – mistaken for extrahepatic obstruction. Check ultrasound: no dilated ducts, but may still have gallstones. The clue: heavy alcohol use + AST>ALT ratio.
- **Acute decompensation in a known cirrhotic** – no fever, minimal jaundice; but alcoholic hepatitis often underdiagnosed in decompensated cirrhosis. Look for rapid rise in bilirubin over 1–2 weeks.
- **Normal AST/ALT** – rare but possible in end-stage liver disease with very low hepatic function. Still consider if history is strong; check GGT (elevated) and bilirubin.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Alcoholic hepatitis is an acute inflammatory injury of the liver caused by heavy alcohol consumption. It sits on a spectrum between simple steatosis and cirrhosis – but unlike fatty liver, it carries a risk of rapid liver failure and death.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Up to 35% of heavy drinkers will develop alcoholic hepatitis at some point; many present to hospital with jaundice.
- **Stakes:** In-hospital mortality ~20–30% for severe disease (Maddrey DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20). If missed, patient can die from hepatorenal syndrome, variceal bleeding, or sepsis.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosed as cholangitis, viral hepatitis, or drug-induced liver injury; empiric antibiotics often started unnecessarily, delaying steroids. Also, failure to calculate severity scores leads to under-treatment.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of Disease Development:**  
Chronic alcohol consumption >40–60 g/day (≈2–3 drinks) for >5 years leads to hepatocyte steatosis. In a subset of patients, a “second hit” (e.g., oxidative stress, acetaldehyde adducts, endotoxemia from gut permeability) triggers a neutrophilic inflammatory response. This is alcoholic hepatitis.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

1. **Acetaldehyde toxicity + CYP2E1 induction** → generation of reactive oxygen species → hepatocyte necrosis → **↑ AST (mitochondrial) and ALT (cytoplasmic)**. Due to pyridoxal-P deficiency from alcohol, ALT is suppressed, giving AST>ALT ratio.

2. **Neutrophil infiltration** (chemotaxis by IL-8, complement activation) → **fever and leukocytosis**. This is sterile – no infection necessary. The neutrophilia correlates with severity.

3. **Hepatocyte ballooning & Mallory-Denk bodies** → **loss of synthetic function** → ↓ albumin, ↑ INR (factor VII first). This explains **jaundice** (bilirubin overload from defective conjugation/excretion) and **coagulopathy**.

4. **Portal hypertension** worsens acutely (sinusoidal inflammation + hepatocyte swelling) → **ascites, splenomegaly**. The swollen liver stretches Glisson’s capsule → **tender hepatomegaly**.

5. **Systemic inflammation** (TNF-α, IL-6) → **anorexia, malaise, low-grade fever**. This also triggers **acute kidney injury** (functional prerenal → hepatorenal syndrome) if severe.

6. **Hepatic encephalopathy** – ammonia bypasses damaged liver; also inflammatory cytokines increase brain sensitivity.

**Connection to Pattern Recognition:**  
The vignette of fever + jaundice + tender liver in a drinker is the direct result of neutrophilic hepatitis. The AST>ALT ratio is specific to this condition (due to pyridoxal-P deficiency). Leukocytosis without obvious infection should immediately raise suspicion for severe alcoholic hepatitis, not start antibiotics.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause / Trigger | 1-Line Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Exogenous** | **Alcohol** (most common) – chronic heavy intake >40–60 g/day for ≥5 years, often recent binge | “He admits to two bottles of wine daily.” |
| **Genetic / Host** | Female sex, obesity, underlying cirrhosis, genetic polymorphisms (PNPLA3, MBOAT7) | “She is obese and drinks only 3–4 units/day but has fatty liver.” |
| **Co-factors** | Smoking, metabolic syndrome, hepatitis C co-infection | “He has been HCV-positive and drinks heavily.” |
| **Infectious Precipitant** | Gut-derived endotoxin (LPS) – triggers TLR4 signalling | “Recent urinary tract infection .” |
| **Idiopathic** | Some without obvious heavy intake – possibly due to genetic vulnerability | “Denies heavy drinking but has AST>ALT ratio 2:1.” (Rare; be cautious) |

**Most Common Cause:** Chronic heavy alcohol consumption – but severity can be triggered by a recent binge or infection. No other common cause produces the same pattern.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria & Scoring Systems:**

- **Maddrey’s Discriminant Function (DF):** 4.6 × (PT – control PT in seconds) + serum bilirubin (mg/dL).  
  - DF <32: mild/moderate (mortality <5%).  
  - DF ≥32: severe (mortality ~30% at 28 days); consider steroids.
- **MELD score:** More accurate for prognosis (bilirubin, INR, creatinine).  
  - MELD ≥20 indicates high risk.
- **Lille Score** (if on steroids): day 7 – combine age, bilirubin, albumin, creatinine, DF.  
  - Lille <0.45 = responder; >0.45 = non-responder → stop steroids.

**Each Test:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|---------------|----------------|
| **LFT (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT)** | Hepatocellular vs cholestatic; AST/ALT ratio | Confirm pattern; exclude obstruction | AST <300, AST/ALT >1.5 (usually >2) = alcoholic hepatitis. ALP may be mildly elevated. GGT elevated (but not specific). |
| **Bilirubin** | Jaundice severity; DF & MELD component | Prognostication | Rising >10 mg/dL in <2 weeks = severe. |
| **PT/INR** | Synthetic function | DF calculation; bleeding risk | INR >1.5 without other cause (Vit K deficiency usually less). |
| **Creatinine** | Renal function | MELD; screen for hepatorenal syndrome | Rising Cr = worse prognosis – may need vasopressors. |
| **CBC (WBC)** | Inflammation | Assess for infection vs sterile neutrophilia | Neutrophilia >10,000 very common; if >15,000 with bands, consider sepsis. But also check CRP. |
| **CRP** | Inflammatory marker | Differentiate from sepsis? (limited) | Usually high (>50 mg/L) in alcoholic hepatitis; but if very high (>150), consider infection. |
| **Ultrasound abdomen** | Liver size, fatty infiltration, exclude obstruction | Rule out biliary obstruction, ascites | No dilated ducts; may show fatty liver and ascites. |
| **Transjugular liver biopsy** | Histology: ballooning, Mallory-Denk, neutrophils | Gold standard but invasive; used when diagnosis uncertain | Not routine – reserved for unclear cases. |

**Single confirmatory finding?** No single test confirms; the triad of heavy alcohol history, AST/ALT >2:1 with AST <300, and neutrophilic leukocytosis ± fever makes the diagnosis highly likely. **Rule out** if patient has no alcohol history or if ALT >500 (think viral hepatitis or DILI).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour):**

- **Stop alcohol –** discuss with patient; offer withdrawal prophylaxis (CIWA protocol – chlordiazepoxide 10–50 mg PO q4h prn). Avoid benzodiazepines if severe hepatic encephalopathy.
- **Calculate DF and MELD –** to stratify severity.
- **Check for infection –** chest X-ray, urine culture, blood cultures. Fever + leukocytosis doesn’t automatically mean sepsis – but if high suspicion (e.g., bands, high procalcitonin), start antibiotics after cultures.
- **Give vitamin K 10 mg IV stat** – to correct any INR due to malnutrition (though rarely enough).

**Ward (24–72h):**

- **Nutrition –** enteral feeding strongly preferred; high-calorie, high-protein (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day). Avoid prolonged NPO.
- **Steroids for severe disease:**
  - Indication: DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20 (excluding active infection, GI bleeding, renal failure).
  - Regimen: Prednisolone 40 mg PO daily for 28 days (or methylprednisolone IV if cannot take oral). Do not use if active infection – must be treated first.
  - Monitor Lille score at day 7: if >0.45, stop steroids (no benefit, worse infection risk).
- **Pentoxifylline:** Historically used, but now not recommended (trials showed no survival benefit).
- **Antibiotics:** Only if documented infection. Prophylactic antibiotics not indicated (risk of fungal overgrowth, *C. diff*).
- **Low threshold for screening for varices** (if no known cirrhosis). Prophylactic beta-blockers not started acutely.
- **Ascites:** Paracentesis if tense; check SAAG >1.1, low protein, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) – give albumin if SBP or diuretic-resistant.

**Long-Term:**

- **Alcohol abstinence –** most important modifiable factor. Offer referral to addiction services, naltrexone (50–100 mg PO daily, avoid in decompensated liver? caution) or acamprosate. Campral is safe in mild-mod liver disease.
- **Vaccination:** Hepatitis A & B, pneumococcus, influenza.
- **Surveillance for HCC:** Ultrasound every 6 months if cirrhosis present.
- **Avoid hepatotoxins:** Acetaminophen ≤2 g/day, NSAIDs (renal risk), statins carefully.
- **Consider liver transplant evaluation** if non-responsive despite abstinence for 6 months (select centers offer early transplant for severe alcoholic hepatitis).

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**

- Don’t start antibiotics just for fever + leukocytosis – reassess after cultures. Sterile neutrophilia is common.
- Don’t wait for biopsy to start steroids if DF ≥32 – higher mortality if delayed.
- Don’t give steroids if GI bleeding is ongoing (risk of perforation).
- Don’t use Vitamin K to “fix” INR in severe liver disease – it won’t work because factor synthesis is impaired.
- Don’t discharge without discussing alcohol cessation – relapse rate >50% without support.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Alcoholic Hepatitis | Acute Viral Hepatitis (Hep B) | Acute Cholangitis | Drug-Induced Liver Injury (DILI) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Mild RUQ, tender hepatomegaly | Often absent or mild RUQ | Severe RUQ pain, colicky, radiating to back | Variable, often none |
| **Exam** | Jaundice, fever, stigmata of CLD, ascites | Jaundice, no ascites (acute), may have hepatomegaly | Jaundice, fever, right upper quadrant tenderness, positive Murphy’s, signs of cholangitis (Charcot’s triad) | Jaundice, may have rash or eosinophilia (if allergic) |
| **ECG** | Nonspecific (sinus tachycardia) | Normal | May show tachycardia | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | AST>ALT (ratio >2), AST <300, ↑GGT, ↑Neutrophils | ALT>AST (ratio <1), AST may be very high (>500), serology: HBsAg+, IgM anti-HBc | ↑ALP, ↑GGT, ↑Bilirubin (direct), ↑WBC with left shift, LFTs cholestatic | Variable; mixed pattern, may have eosinophilia, INR may be elevated |
| **Key Diff** | Heavy alcohol history + AST/ALT ratio >2 + Neutrophilia | Serology positive; ALT >500; no alcohol history | Dilated bile ducts on US; Charcot’s triad (pain, fever, jaundice) | Temporal link to drug; often hepatocellular (ALT > AST) or cholestatic; acute onset |

**Mimic 4: Decompensated cirrhosis without acute hepatitis** – no fever, minimal AST/ALT change, more chronic stigmata, bilirubin often lower but rising gradually.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Ascites / SBP** | Increasing abdominal girth, abdominal pain, fever | Shifting dullness, paracentesis: SAAG >1.1, neutrophils >250/μL | Paracentesis; if SBP: third-generation cephalosporin (cefotaxime 2g IV q8h) + albumin 1.5 g/kg IV day 1, then 1 g/kg day 3 |
| **Variceal Bleeding** | Haematemesis, melena, hypotension | Stigmata of portal hypertension; emergency OGD | Resuscitate, octreotide 50 μg bolus + 50 μg/h infusion, antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV), band ligation |
| **Hepatic Encephalopathy** | Confusion, asterixis, sleep-wake reversal, coma | Flapping tremor, ammonia elevated (not diagnostic of severity), precipitant: infection, GI bleed, electrolyte | Lactulose 20 g PO/NG q6h (orally/pr), adjust to 2–3 soft stools; rifaximin if non-responsive; treat precipitant |
| **Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS)** | Oliguria, rising creatinine/urea, hyponatraemia | No proteinuria, urine sodium <10, no improvement with volume expansion | Stop diuretics; give albumin 20–40 g/day; if HRS type I: terlipressin (or norepinephrine) + albumin; consider TIPS or transplant |
| **Infections (bacterial/fungal)** | Any change in vitals, worsening encephalopathy | High CRP, procalcitonin, positive cultures | Empiric antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam) if systemic signs; fungal prophylaxis with fluconazole if steroids used and high risk |
| **Pancreatitis** | Epigastric pain, vomiting, lipase >3x normal | Co-existing acute pancreatitis (common in alcoholics) | Supportive: NPO, IVF, avoid steroids if pancreatitis is acute (relative contraindication) |

**Late-presentation patterns (stems testing “what if untreated”):**  
- Jaundice worsens, but patient develops ascites and confusion → hepatic encephalopathy + HRS → oliguria and anuria → death from multi-organ failure.  
- Or first bleed from varices → massive haematemesis → shock, aspiration → death.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **AH – Alcoholic Hepatitis Assessment:**
   - **A** – Alcohol history (chronic heavy)
   - **S** – Symptom onset (weeks)
   - **T** – Transaminases AST>ALT
   - **E** – Encephalopathy? / Elevated INR
   - **R** – Ratio (AST/ALT >2)
   
2. **SEVERE** for risk factors:  
   - **S** – Female sex  
   - **E** – Elevated bilirubin (>10)  
   - **V** – Variceal bleeding (or portal HT)  
   - **E** – Elevated INR (>1.5)  
   - **R** – Renal failure  
   - **E** – Encephalopathy

3. **Maddrey DF calculation: 4.6 × (PT – control) + bilirubin (mg/dL)** – remember 4.6 is like “four six-pack” of beer.

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls (Pattern-Recognition Cues):**

- **AST > ALT with AST <300** is *almost* pathognomonic for alcoholic hepatitis. Viral hepatitis usually >500.
- **Fever + leukocytosis + jaundice** in a drinker: do not start antibiotics – first check DF. Sterile neutrophilia responds to steroids, not antibiotics.
- **If INR does not correct after vitamin K** and bilirubin is high, think alcoholic hepatitis, not simple cholestasis.
- **Spider naevi + palmar erythema** = chronic liver disease; acute alcoholic hepatitis adds acute deterioration.
- **A rising bilirubin despite improvement in other parameters** may indicate early sepsis (SBP) rather than worsening hepatitis.
- **Steroids only if no active infection** – always screen with CXR, urine, blood cultures, ascitic tap before starting.
- **Transjugular biopsy is reserved** when diagnosis is unclear (e.g., no obvious alcohol history, atypical labs). Most cases diagnosed clinically.

**Common Exam/Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- Using ALT >500 to rule out alcoholic hepatitis – viral hepatitis must be excluded first.
- Giving steroids to a patient with GI bleeding – increased risk of perforation.
- Relying on pentoxifylline – now shown to be ineffective.
- Not calculating Lille score at day 7 – many unnecessary prolonged steroid courses.
- Forgetting alcohol withdrawal prophylaxis: seizures can be fatal in a patient with coagulopathy.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

- **NEWS2 ≥7** – high risk of deterioration; escalate to critical care review.
- **GCS ≤12** – indicates severe encephalopathy; risk of airway compromise → ICU for intubation.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests severe tissue hypoperfusion (consider sepsis or bleeding).
- **Creatinine rising >0.3 mg/dL per day** – early HRS; needs senior review (nephrology/hepatology).
- **DF ≥32 and not responding to steroids** (Lille >0.45, bilirubin not dropping) – consider second-line therapies (e.g., G-CSF? early transplant discussion).
- **Active variceal bleeding** – call endoscopy immediately (out-of-hours: on-call GI team). If hemodynamic instability, involve ICU.
- **INR >2.5 with active bleeding** – reversal needed: FFP or prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) – but avoid over-correction.
- **SBP confirmed** – if patient becomes hypotensive, escalate for vasopressors.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy grade III/IV** – ICU admission for airway protection.
- **Any new-onset atrial fibrillation** (common in alcoholic hepatitis from electrolyte disturbance or alcohol withdrawal) – cardiology consult.

**Specific Thresholds to Remember:**
- MELD ≥20: consider steroids + ICU monitoring.
- DF ≥32: severe – start steroids, consider ICU if other organ failures.
- Lille score after 7 days >0.45: stop steroids – no benefit and increased infection risk.
- Bilirubin plateau >20 mg/dL for >2 weeks: likely non-response; arrange transplant evaluation.

**Always have a low threshold to involve a hepatologist or transplant centre early – severe alcoholic hepatitis is a high-mortality emergency, not a “just stop drinking” condition.**
